questions.scenario: A beginner opens Cargo.toml and sees name, version, edition, and dependencies. What is Cargo.toml doing?

options.text: Describing the package metadata and dependency requests Cargo should use.

questions.explanation: Cargo.toml is the manifest: package metadata plus dependency requests.

questions.scenario: An application has dependencies and a Cargo.lock file. Why should the app usually commit Cargo.lock?

options.text: It records exact dependency versions so CI and teammates build the same graph.
It is the only place Rust source code can live.
It is required because src/main.rs cannot compile without comments.

questions.explanation: For applications and tools, Cargo.lock makes dependency movement visible and reproducible.

questions.scenario: Which statements match the articles Cargo vocabulary?

options.text: A package is the project Cargo manages and is described by Cargo.toml.
A crate is one compiled Rust unit, such as a library or executable.
A crate root is usually src/main.rs or src/lib.rs.
A registry is the local function where main starts.

questions.explanation: Package, crate, and crate root match the vocabulary table. A registry is where Cargo can get crates, usually crates.io.
